questionText: The curve $C$ has equation $x + \ln y = x^2$, where $y > 0$. (a) Show that $\frac{dy}{dx} = y(2x - 1)$. (b) Find an equation of the tangent to $C$ at the point $(1, 1)$. (c) Find, in exact form, the coordinates of the point on $C$ at which the tangent is horizontal. (d) Find an equation of the normal to $C$ at $(1, 1)$, and explain why $C$ has no point with a vertical tangent.

steps.description: Differentiate both sides with respect to x
steps.explanation: We differentiate the whole equation, treating $y$ as a function of $x$.

steps.description: Differentiate the ln y term with the chain rule
steps.explanation: By the chain rule, $\ln y$ differentiates to $\frac{1}{y}$ times $\frac{dy}{dx}$ because $y$ depends on $x$.
The term $x$ gives $1$ and $x^2$ gives $2x$, producing one equation containing $\frac{dy}{dx}$.
Subtracting $1$ from both sides leaves the $\frac{dy}{dx}$ term alone on the left.
Multiplying both sides by $y$ gives the required expression, proving part (a).
Since $\ln 1 = 0$, the point $(1,1)$ satisfies the equation and lies on $C$.
Substituting $x=1,\,y=1$ into $y(2x-1)$ gives the tangent gradient $1$.
Using $y-y_1=m(x-x_1)$ with $m=1$ through $(1,1)$ gives the tangent $y=x$. This answers part (b).

steps.description: Set up the horizontal tangent condition
steps.explanation: A horizontal tangent has gradient $0$, so the product $y(2x-1)$ must be zero.
Since $y>0$ everywhere on $C$, the only way the product is zero is $2x-1=0$, giving $x=\frac12$.
The horizontal-tangent point lies on $C$, so we substitute $x=\frac12$ into the equation.
This is the exact point at which the tangent is horizontal. This answers part (c).
The normal gradient is the negative reciprocal of $1$, namely $-1$; the line through $(1,1)$ is $x+y=2$.

steps.description: Explain why there is no vertical tangent
steps.workingLatex: \frac{dy}{dx} = y(2x - 1) \text{ is finite for every point on } C
steps.explanation: The gradient is a product of finite quantities and is defined for all points on $C$, so it is never infinite; hence $C$ has no vertical tangent. This completes part (d).

workedSolution.finalAnswer: (a) $\frac{dy}{dx} = y(2x - 1)$; (b) tangent $y = x$; (c) horizontal tangent at $\left(\frac{1}{2}, e^{-1/4}\right)$; (d) normal $x + y = 2$; since $\frac{dy}{dx} = y(2x - 1)$ is finite everywhere on $C$, there is no vertical tangent.
